items.myth: Every outlier is a mistake and should be deleted.
items.reality: Many outliers are valid rare events—the goal is to decide whether the point is noise or signal, not to maximize 'normal-looking' data.
items.example: A single 9.0 earthquake reading in a seismic dataset is rare but real; deleting it would erase the event the model should detect.

items.myth: If |z| ≤ 3, the point is definitely not an outlier.
items.reality: The 3σ rule is a heuristic for roughly normal data; skewed, discrete, or multi-modal features break the link between z-scores and 'unusual'.
items.example: Bimodal clusters can place typical points from the smaller cluster beyond 3σ of the global mean even though they are not errors.

items.myth: Tree models never care about outliers, so preprocessing is unnecessary.
items.reality: Splits on raw scales can still be suboptimal for linear margins and derived features; linear heads, neural nets, and distance-based steps remain sensitive.
items.example: A gradient-boosted tree may handle raw price outliers, but a log-transformed feature often yields cleaner splits and better calibration for skewed spend.
